content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

July 6, 1977

Floyd Shimomura
Deputy Attorney General
555 Capitol Mall, Suite 550
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: AG Opinion Request
    No. CV 77/102 IL

Dear Floyd:

    This letter is in response to your request for the
Commission's views on Attorney General Opinion Request No.
CV 77/102 IL. We concur with your decision to answer this
request with an indexed letter and request that you contact us
if you decide to answer the question in a published opinion.

    We will comment only on questions 2, 3 and 4 posed
by Mr. Davis since question 1 does not call for an interpreta-
tion of the Political Reform Act. The second question concerns
whether an official has received a gift within the meaning of
the Political Reform Act by accepting food and lodging from a
friend. Government Code Section 82028/ defines the term "gift"
to include

    ...any payment to the extent that consideration
    of equal or greater value is not received.

The term "payment" is defined in Section 82044 to include

    ...a payment, distribution, transfer, loan,
    advance, deposit, gift or other rendering of
    money, property, services or anything else of
    value, whether tangible or intangible.

Thus, food and lodging provided to an official by a friend will
constitute a gift to him unless he has provided consideration of
equal or greater value in return.

_________________
1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code
unless otherwise noted.

Floyd Shimomura
Page two
July 6, 1977

Mr. Davis' third question concerns the issue of
whether reciprocal services constitute full and adequate consideration. The Commission has provided by regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 13727 that any consideration including entertainment or a household gift of a reasonably similar benefit or value is sufficient. Commission regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18630 sets forth stricter standards if the donor is a lobbyist. Specifically, the lobbyist may not deduct or be reimbursed by his employer for the cost of providing the home hospitality and the reciprocity must be provided to the lobbyist during the same reporting period in which he provided the official with the hospitality.

The thrust of Mr. Davis' fourth question appears to be whether the food and lodging may be considered a gift to the state rather than to the official personally if he does not accept his state per diem. The Commission recently adopted the enclosed opinion requested by Peter G. Stone, City Attorney of San Jose (No. 77-003) in which it set forth the standards for determining whether a gift is made to an officer or a government agency. On the opinion requested by Kenneth Cory, FPPC Opinions 153 (No. 75-094-b, Oct. 23, 1975), the Commission stated that services provided as an incident of neighborliness did not constitute reportable gifts. However, this standard does not apply to gifts of food and lodging since the Commission has specifically addressed this area of reporting in Regulation 18727. Moreover, it is unlikely that food and lodging would ever be a gift solely to the agency pursuant to the standards set forth in the Stone opinion since to do so the donor would, on the public record, have to make his home available to all members of the government agency without regard to rank or position and give the agency substantial control over his home.

Thus, in practice, whether or not an official claims per diem from the state, he will have received a reportable gift when he accepts food and lodging from a friend unless he provides reciprocal consideration of approximately similar benefit or value.

_____________

2/ Officials in the Governor's Office must report gifts and income pursuant to the conflict of interest code for the Governor's Office. Executive Order No. B2376, Section 6, provides that all pertinent regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are applicable to the conflict of interest code for the Governor's Office.

Floyd Shimomura
Page three
July 6, 1977


If you have any further questions, please do not hesi-
tate to contact either me or Ted Prim, an attorney with the Com-
mission's Legal Division.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Michael Bennett
                                        Executive Director


MB:TF:jo

Enclosures
